scenarios.task: A lead has reported this task as '90% complete' for the third status cycle in a row, based on how much time has been spent on it relative to the original estimate.
criteria: Flags reporting percent-complete based on time/effort elapsed rather than objective completion criteria as the failure mode
States that earned value should be tied to objective, pre-defined completion criteria per task, not duration consumed or a lead's gut feel
Recommends asking which specific defined sub-tasks or milestones are actually done rather than accepting a confidence-based percentage
Names this as the '90% done for three reports running' pattern explicitly

scenarios.task: The project is facing a cost overrun that exceeds my delegated contingency authority. I'm planning to just draw down from the sponsor's management reserve directly to cover it, since it's the same pool of contingency-type money conceptually.
criteria: Flags treating management reserve as something the specialist can authorize as the failure mode
States that contingency reserve is money the specialist spends deliberately against named risks, while management reserve is not theirs to authorize
Recommends routing the request to the sponsor/CCB rather than drawing on management reserve directly
States that blurring the two either invites unapproved raids on the sponsor's reserve or misrepresents what's actually being spent

scenarios.task: Having learned that adding headcount to a late project usually backfires, I'm refusing a request to bring in a narrow-scope contractor for a single, well-isolated integration fix with minimal ramp-up needed, treating it the same as adding general engineering headcount to a broadly late project.
criteria: Flags refusing the narrow-scope addition on reflex as the overcorrecting-on-Brookss-Law failure mode
States that Brooks's Law is about coordination overhead swamping throughput on broad, ongoing work, not a categorical ban on any additional resourcing
Recommends evaluating the narrow, well-isolated contractor addition on its own merits rather than reflexively refusing it
Distinguishes a specialist brought in for one isolated task from adding general headcount to a broadly late team

scenarios.task: The project's variance is looking bad against the original baseline, so I've quietly adjusted the plan/baseline figures to make the numbers look more reasonable, without running it through change control or logging an approved rebaseline event.
criteria: Flags quietly adjusting the baseline without formal change control as the silent-rebaselining failure mode
States that rebaselining must be a discrete, dated, approved event, and skipping that corrupts every EVM variance number that follows
Recommends running any baseline change through the proper change control process with sponsor/CCB approval and logging it
Notes this is exactly the mechanism that lets a bad variance disappear from the record without genuine resolution
